extracted_rules.condition: When establishing FTP for a new athlete or after a training block of 6+ weeks
extracted_rules.action: Perform a 20-minute all-out time trial; multiply average power by 0.95 to estimate FTP
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: FTP = 20min_avg_power × 0.95; alternatively ramp test: last completed 1-min stage × 0.75; retest every 6-8 weeks
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter; ramp test Borszcz et al. 2020

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling an active recovery ride
extracted_rules.action: Ride at <55% FTP; minimal physiological stress; used for recovery between hard sessions
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: <55% FTP; HR typically <68% HRmax; RPE 1-2; no training adaptation targeted
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter (Coggan power zones)

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling an aerobic endurance ride
extracted_rules.action: Ride at 55-74% FTP; primary zone for aerobic base building; forms 60-70% of total cycling volume in base phase
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 55-74% FTP; HR ~69-83% HRmax; RPE 2-3; lactate <2 mmol/L
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling a tempo ride for aerobic threshold development
extracted_rules.action: Ride at 75-89% FTP; sustainable for 60-120 minutes in trained cyclists; limit to ≤20% of weekly volume
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 75-89% FTP; HR ~84-94% HRmax; RPE 4-5; lactate 2-4 mmol/L; volume cap ≤20% weekly
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter

extracted_rules.action: Ride at 90-104% FTP; typical interval duration 8-20 minutes; total Z4 volume ≤10% of weekly volume
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 90-104% FTP; HR ~94-100% LTHR; RPE 6-7; sustainable for 60 min at FTP; interval duration 8-20 min
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling VO2max intervals on the bike
extracted_rules.action: Ride at 105-120% FTP; intervals of 3-8 minutes; rest:work ratio 1:1; limit to ≤8% of weekly volume
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 105-120% FTP; HR >100% LTHR; RPE 8; interval duration 3-8 min; rest = work duration; volume ≤8% weekly
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling anaerobic capacity or sprint preparation intervals
extracted_rules.action: Ride at 121-150% FTP; very short intervals (30 sec to 2 min); long recovery (1:4 to 1:6 work:rest); limit to ≤5% of weekly volume
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: 121-150% FTP; RPE 9-10; interval duration 30s-2min; work:rest 1:4 to 1:6; volume ≤5% weekly
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter

extracted_rules.condition: When tracking long-term aerobic fitness and training load for a cyclist
extracted_rules.action: Calculate CTL as 42-day EWMA of daily TSS; this represents aerobic fitness baseline
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: CTL = 42-day EWMA of daily TSS; λ = 2/(42+1) ≈ 0.046; typical competitive cyclist CTL: 60-100 TSS/day
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter; Banister 1991 impulse-response model

extracted_rules.condition: When tracking short-term fatigue accumulation for a cyclist
extracted_rules.action: Calculate ATL as 7-day EWMA of daily TSS; represents current fatigue state
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: ATL = 7-day EWMA of daily TSS; λ = 2/(7+1) = 0.25; ATL > CTL indicates accumulated fatigue
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter; Banister 1991

extracted_rules.condition: When assessing athlete readiness for racing or peak performance
extracted_rules.action: Compute TSB = CTL − ATL; positive TSB indicates freshness; target +5 to +25 for A-race day
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: TSB = CTL − ATL; target for A-race: +5 to +25; <−20: significant fatigue; >+25: risk of detraining
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter

extracted_rules.rule_name: HIIT superiority for VO2max in cycling
extracted_rules.condition: If the goal is to improve VO2max or maximal aerobic power in cyclists
extracted_rules.action: Prioritize HIIT over moderate-to-vigorous continuous training (MVICT); HIIT produces larger gains in relative VO2max and maximal aerobic power
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: HIIT vs MVICT: VO2max relative g=0.39; VO2max absolute g=0.29; maximal aerobic power g=0.31; anaerobic power g=0.47
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: HIIT vs MVICT across 115 trials (original biking_coach_power_rules source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Caffeine dose for general cycling performance
extracted_rules.condition: If a cyclist uses caffeine to improve general cycling performance (time-to-completion reduction)
extracted_rules.action: Use a low dose of caffeine (~1h before effort); low dose more effective than high dose for completion-time performance
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Dose: ≤3 mg/kg body weight; timing: ~60 min pre-exercise; effect: SMD=-0.36 on time-to-completion, SMD=+0.29 on mean power
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: caffeine and cycling performance (original biking_coach_power_rules source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Caffeine dose for sprint repeat performance (RSA)
extracted_rules.condition: If a cyclist targets repeated sprint ability (RSA) improvement with caffeine
extracted_rules.action: Use a higher caffeine dose for RSA; effect is larger in cycling than running RSA protocols
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Dose: ≥6 mg/kg body weight; RSA peak power improvement: WMD +6.67 (cycling) vs +4.56 (running); timing: 60 min pre-effort
extracted_rules.source: Meta-analysis: caffeine and repeated sprint ability (original biking_coach_power_rules source)

extracted_rules.condition: When prescribing cadence targets for cycling sessions
extracted_rules.action: Vary target cadence by session type; higher cadence for endurance, lower for strength intervals
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Endurance/Z2-Z3: 85-100 rpm; threshold/Z4: 85-95 rpm; strength intervals/Z6: 60-80 rpm; sprint: 100-120 rpm
extracted_rules.source: Cycling biomechanics research; Abbiss et al. 2009 cadence optimization

extracted_rules.rule_name: Bike fit — lower back pain prevention
extracted_rules.condition: If a cyclist reports lower back pain or discomfort
extracted_rules.action: Perform a personalized dynamic bike fit adjusted to workload demands; static fits are insufficient for preventing LBP under varied training loads
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Dynamic bike fit (workload-adapted): significant LBP reduction vs. no fit; saddle height, fore-aft position, and handlebar drop are primary adjustment vectors
extracted_rules.source: Systematic review: bike fitting and lower back pain in cyclists (original biking_coach_power_rules source)

extracted_rules.rule_name: Weekly TSS load thresholds by training category
extracted_rules.condition: When classifying weekly training stress for a cyclist
extracted_rules.action: Categorize weekly TSS to determine training stress level and OTS risk; sustained >600 TSS/week for >3 weeks requires monitoring for non-functional overreaching
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: <300 TSS/wk: minimal aerobic load; 300-450: moderate training stress; 450-600: high training stress (well-adapted athletes); >600: very high — OTS risk if sustained >3 weeks without deload; peak CTL builders: 800-1000 TSS/wk (professional endurance athletes only)
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter; TSS application in applied cycling coaching

extracted_rules.condition: If scheduling sprint or explosive power efforts on the bike
extracted_rules.action: Ride at >150% FTP for very short sprints (<30 seconds); HR is not a useful metric (too slow to respond); track only as peak power in watts and peak power-to-weight ratio
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: >150% FTP; efforts <30s; HR: not quantifiable (lag too large); primary metric: peak power watts + W/kg; typical elite sprint: 15-23 W/kg for 5-second peak; recreational: 10-14 W/kg
extracted_rules.source: Coggan & Allen, Training and Racing with a Power Meter (Zone 7 definition); sprint physiology research

extracted_rules.rule_name: FTP test selection — ramp vs 20-minute based on fatigue state
extracted_rules.condition: When choosing between the ramp test and the 20-minute FTP test
extracted_rules.action: Prefer ramp test for fatigued athletes, mid-block assessments, or frequent retesting; use 20-minute test when fully rested (taper state) for most accurate absolute FTP; both are valid but bias differently
extracted_rules.formula_or_value: Ramp test: FTP = last_completed_minute_avg_power × 0.75; suitable for fatigued states; tends to overestimate FTP slightly for track/sprint-biased athletes; 20-min test: FTP = avg_power × 0.95; best when fully rested; tends to underestimate for long-course athletes
extracted_rules.source: Borszcz et al. 2020 ramp test validity; FTP assessment comparison research; Coggan & Allen practical guidance
